abstract: In this study, we developed an aryl amino-substituted graphitic carbon nitride (g-C₃N₄) catalyst featuring atomically dispersed Mn sites, designed for the direct synthesis of hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂) from seawater. The catalyst demonstrates exceptional reactivity, producing up to 2230 μM of H₂O₂ in alkaline water and 1800 μM in seawater over a 7-hour period. Furthermore, the material exhibits high stability and ease of recovery, maintaining its performance over multiple cycles. Mechanistic investigations reveal that rather than following the conventional two-electron oxygen reduction pathway, H₂O₂ generation proceeds via a two-electron water oxidation reaction (WOR). This process involves both a direct route, where photoinduced holes (h⁺) oxidize H₂O in a single step, and an indirect route, where h⁺ oxidizes hydroxide ions (OH⁻) to form hydroxyl radicals (•OH) that subsequently dimerize into H₂O₂. We employed a comprehensive suite of analytical techniques—including electron paramagnetic resonance, X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XANES/EXAFS), high-resolution TEM, XPS, and solid-state NMR—alongside multiscale molecular modeling and reactive molecular dynamics to characterize the catalytic centers at the atomic level and elucidate the underlying reaction dynamics.
